title: Inside Climate News
description: Pulitzer Prize-winning, nonpartisan reporting on the biggest crisis facing our planet.

items.title: Coral Reefs in French Polynesia Are Stuck Between Life and Death
items.description: This story was supported by the Pulitzer Center. On the island of Moorea, in French Polynesia, deep green volcanic mountains slope from high peaks down towards the sea. But they don’t stop at the shore. The ridgelines of ancient lava flows stretch underwater, providing the perfect rocky substrate for a coral reef. Between the ancient […]

items.title: Why Wildfire Experts Are So Worried About This Year’s Fire Season
items.description: As bad as things got in Los Angeles in January 2025, when 31 people died and more than 16,000 buildings were destroyed by wildfires roaring into residential neighborhoods, many wildland firefighters look back on the rest of last year as a dodged bullet.  Across the nation, according to the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC), which […]

items.title: A Georgia Wildlife Haven Forged by Fire and Peat Nears UNESCO Recognition
items.description: FOLKSTON, Ga.—The world’s smallest heron hops from blade to blade in a patch of tall grass, testing its footing above the dark water as it searches for an evening meal. “This was already worth the trip out today,” Joshua Howard said earlier this month from a gray flat-bottomed tour boat just a few yards away. […]

items.title: Greenpeace Plans to Sue JBS for Its Climate Impacts, Seeks Details About Major Plans in Nigeria
items.description: The world’s largest meat company is preparing to build a sprawling industrial beef operation in Nigeria—its first on the African continent—but has not revealed details about its plans, prompting a challenge by environmental advocates. JBS, the Brazilian beef juggernaut plagued by corruption scandals and ongoing links to deforestation in the Amazon rainforest, announced its first […]

items.title: The Okefenokee’s Bid for International Recognition
items.description: A vast swamp in southeast Georgia, the Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge is an environmental wonderland: a protected wilderness of blackwater channels, peat and dense wetland forests, supporting a rich array of wildlife and plant life.  By July, the Okefenokee could be internationally recognized as a United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World Heritage […]

items.title: The 2026 World Cup Will Feature a Villainous Player: Extreme Heat
items.description: Sávio Bortolini Pimentel just missed getting on the roster to represent his national team, Brazil, at the 1994 FIFA World Cup in the United States. At the time, he was a 20-year-old professional player with the Rio de Janeiro team Flamengo. He recalls other players telling him after the fact that the weather during some […]

items.title: Air Monitors Used in California Tank Crisis Were Inadequate in the Past, Leaving Returning Residents Uneasy
items.description: GARDEN GROVE, Calif.—The day before his 41st birthday, Hernando Morales found himself hurrying his one-year-old into the backseat of his car when an industrial tank just over a mile away from his apartment threatened to explode and release toxic chemicals throughout the area. Five days later, local officials said it was safe and his family […]

items.title: As Seas Rise, Louisiana Faces a Choice: Plan for Movement or Let Crisis Decide
items.description: The shoreline of Louisiana has never been still or fixed, though recent generations have treated it as such. Since the last ice age roughly 20,000 years ago, around when people arrived in what is now the United States, sea levels have repeatedly reshaped aspects of the Gulf Coast. But today, human-caused warming is accelerating that […]

items.title: Let Terry Tempest Williams Teach You How to Find Your Own Glorians
items.description: From our collaborating partner Living on Earth, public radio’s environmental news magazine, an interview by host Steve Curwood with author Terry Tempest Williams.  At first glance, a desert may appear barren. But it’s actually a place teeming with life. There are coyotes, wind in the cottonwood trees, a never-ending night sky, and once in a […]

items.title: Scientists Outplant Experimental ‘Flonduran’ Corals in Florida’s Dry Tortugas National Park
items.description: Nearly three dozen young lab-grown elkhorn corals were outplanted onto reefs in Florida’s Dry Tortugas National Park this spring, including a group of “Flondurans,” marking the first time this experimental cross-breed of Florida and Honduran elkhorn corals was introduced to the remote park about 70 miles from Key West. “These babies have been raised on […]
